coredata.dc:title: Surface restructuring Prussian blue analog-derived bimetallic CoFe phosphides by N-doped graphene quantum dots for electroactive hydrogen evolving catalyst
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Wei-Shiang
Rinawati, Mia
Huang, Wei-Hsiang
Chang, Chia-Yu
Chang, Ling-Yu
Cheng, Yao-Sheng
Chang, Ching-Cheng
Chen, Jeng-Lung
Su, Wei-Nien
Yeh, Min-Hsin
coredata.dc:description: As a crucial stage of electrochemical water splitting, hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) favour catalyst to attain rapid kinetics for its broader application, alternating Pt in the acidic environment. Transition metal phosphides (TMPs) are one kind of earth-abundant, nonprecious-based catalyst which has been classified as a viable alternative and active for HER. While the performance remains inferior to Pt which primarily targets durability under high current density, pinpointing the reconfiguration strategy would be critical to their catalytic competency. Herein, we reported engineered N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD) on the surface of bimetallic CoFe phosphide (CoFeP) derived from cobalt iron Prussian blue analogue (CoFePBA) as an efficient HER. By introducing the NGQD, the surface architect and electronic state of the transition metal are altered through an adjusted electronic configuration and thus, improving the electrocatalytic activity for HER. The X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) highlighting the role of NGQD, which successfully induced the electron density of Co atoms, further expedites its conductivity and electroactivity. The optimized NGQD/CoFeP substantially surpasses an overpotential of 70 mV (vs. RHE) at the current density of 10 mA cm−2 in 0.5 M H2SO4. Furthermore, the NGQD/CoFeP maintains its exceptional stability under an extremely high current density of 600 mA cm−2 after 12 h of continuous operation. Our findings show that NGQD/CoFeP might demonstrate as a viable alternative to the conventional Pt electrocatalyst in commercial water splitting for hydrogen generation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrocatalyst
Hydrogen evolution reaction
N-doped graphene quantum dots
Prussian blue analogues
Transition metal phosphides
X-ray absorption spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrocatalytic activities of CoFe PBA with various NGQD amount toward hydrogen evolution reaction
Electrochemical performance of NGQD/CoFeP for hydrogen evolution reaction

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Taiwan University of Science and Technology & Institut Teknologi Sepuluh Nopember

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) in Taiwan (MOST 111-2221-E-011-011, NSTC 112-2218-E-01-011, and NSTC 112-2221-E-011-016-MY3), the Ministry of Education of Taiwan (“Sustainable Electrochemical Energy Development (SEED) Center” project) and the National Taiwan University of Science and Technology & Institut Teknologi Sepuluh Nopember Joint Research Program (ITS-NTUST-112-05). Research facilities of hard-XAS and soft-XAS were provided by the beamline of BL17C1 and BL20A1, respectively, National Synchrotron Radiation Research Center (NSRRC) in Taiwan. We also thank Ms. C.-Y. Chien of NSTC (located at National Taiwan University) for assistance with the TEM experiments.

This work was financially supported by the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) in Taiwan (MOST 111-2221-E-011-011, NSTC 112-2218-E-01-011, and NSTC 112-2221-E-011-016-MY3), the Ministry of Education of Taiwan (“Sustainable Electrochemical Energy Development (SEED) Center” project) and the National Taiwan University of Science and Technology & Institut Teknologi Sepuluh Nopember Joint Research Program (ITS-NTUST-112-05). Research facilities of hard-XAS and soft-XAS were provided by the beamline of BL17C1 and BL20A1, respectively, National Synchrotron Radiation Research Center (NSRRC) in Taiwan. We also thank Ms. C.-Y. Chien of NSTC (located at National Taiwan University) for assistance with the TEM experiments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM image of NGQD and the particle size distribution; (b) PL excitation-emission mapping of NGQD; the high-resolution XPS spectra of NGQD for (c) C 1 s and (d) N 1 s.
The SEM image of (a) CoFePBA and (b) NGQD/CoFePBA; (c) Co L2,3-edge and (d) Fe L2,3-edge XAS spectra of CoFePBA and NGQD/CoFePBA.

(a) X-ray absorption spectra of Co K-edge of CoFePBA and NGQD/CoFePBA (Co3O4 and Co foil are reference); (b) X-ray absorption spectra of Fe K-edge of CoFePBA and NGQD/CoFePBA (Fe3O4 and Fe foil are reference).
(a) The TEM image of NGQD/CoFeP and EDX elemental mapping of elemental: (b) Co (c) Fe (d) P (e) O (f) C (g) N.
(a) The XPS survey spectra of NGQD/CoFeP and high-solution XPS spectra of (b) Co 2p, (c) Fe 2p, (d) P 2p.
(a) Polarization curves (b) Tafel plots (c) Nyquist plots of CoFePBA, 0.1NGQD/CoFePBA, 1NGQD/CoFePBA, and 3NGQD/CoFePBA in 0.5 M H2SO4.

(a) Polarization curves, (b) Tafel plots, and (c) Nyquist plots of NGQD/CoFeP, CoFeP, NGQD/CoFePBA, CoFePBA, and Pt/C in 0.5 M H2SO4; (d) The long-term stability of NGQD/CoFeP at 600 mA cm-2 for 12 h.

(a) The figure of the H-type cell module and drainage-air collection device conducted for the H2 purity test; (b) The result of GC data for H2 purity test; (c) Faradaic efficiency of NGQD/CoFeP operating in H-type cell at 100 mA cm-2 for 30 min.
The synthetic procedure of bimetallic CoFe PBA derived NGQD/CoFeP on Ni foam.

head.ce:title: Surface restructuring Prussian blue analog-derived bimetallic CoFe phosphides by N-doped graphene quantum dots for electroactive hydrogen evolving catalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei 10607, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei 10607, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Synchrotron Radiation Research Center, Hsinchu 30076, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Synchrotron Radiation Research Center, Hsinchu 30076, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate Institute of Applied Science and Technology, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei 10607, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graduate Institute of Applied Science and Technology
National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Graduate Institute of Applied Science and Technology, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei 10607, Taiwan

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a crucial stage of electrochemical water splitting, hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) favour catalyst to attain rapid kinetics for its broader application, alternating Pt in the acidic environment. Transition metal phosphides (TMPs) are one kind of earth-abundant, nonprecious-based catalyst which has been classified as a viable alternative and active for HER. While the performance remains inferior to Pt which primarily targets durability under high current density, pinpointing the reconfiguration strategy would be critical to their catalytic competency. Herein, we reported engineered N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD) on the surface of bimetallic CoFe phosphide (CoFeP) derived from cobalt iron Prussian blue analogue (CoFePBA) as an efficient HER. By introducing the NGQD, the surface architect and electronic state of the transition metal are altered through an adjusted electronic configuration and thus, improving the electrocatalytic activity for HER. The X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) highlighting the role of NGQD, which successfully induced the electron density of Co atoms, further expedites its conductivity and electroactivity. The optimized NGQD/CoFeP substantially surpasses an overpotential of 70 mV (vs. RHE) at the current density of 10 mA cm−2 in 0.5 M H2SO4. Furthermore, the NGQD/CoFeP maintains its exceptional stability under an extremely high current density of 600 mA cm−2 after 12 h of continuous operation. Our findings show that NGQD/CoFeP might demonstrate as a viable alternative to the conventional Pt electrocatalyst in commercial water splitting for hydrogen generation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Excessive usage of fossil fuels is rapidly deteriorating environmental issues including air pollution and global warming, both of which are amplified by the world's expanding population and burgeoning industrial sector [1]. Scientists these days devotes a considerable amount of effort to creating new renewable energy sources. Hydrogen fuel, by its high energy density and purity, has attracted widespread interest among numerous renewable resources [2]. Currently, most of this hydrogen is accustomed to industrial desires, such as producing ammonia for fertilizers and refining petroleum and other chemicals [3]. At present, there are three main routes for industrial hydrogen production, including steam methane reforming, coal gasification, and water electrolysis [4]. Natural gas steam reforming remains the primary method for producing commercial hydrogen [5], however, the entire process still requires fossil fuels that would produce carbon dioxide during the reaction. Compared with natural gas steam reforming, hydrogen may be made from water electrolysisin a sustainable, environmentally friendly, and more effective way than conventional technologies. As a result, water electrolysisis thought to be the most innovative sustainable hydrogen production method.

Water splitting involves the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER). Given the sluggish kinetics of the reactions, the HER is only effectively promoted by the Pt-based catalyst, which suffers from the minimal availability and pricey [6]. Hence, it is necessary to develop catalysts with high efficiency and stability that can simultaneously diminish the overpotential and accelerate up the reaction. In the past few years, a number of transition-metal compounds [7], including metal alloys, phosphides [8,9], borides [10,11], carbides [12,13], selenides [14,15], and sulphides [16–18] have been found as viable prospective HER electrocatalysts. Iron, cobalt, nickel, molybdenum, and tungsten phosphides are among the most recently studied of these materials due to their strong electrocatalytic performance and environmental friendliness [19–22]. The inherent benefits of transition metal phosphide include well conductivity, affordable production, outstanding electrochemical stability, the capacity to alter metal species, acceptable reaction intermediate binding energy, and so on [2,23,24]. Nevertheless, past studies show that during the phosphating process, the metal center ion is transformed into metal phosphide while the organic ligand is transformed into amorphous carbon, both of which have poor conductivity and significant load transfer resistance [25,26]. Notably, the introduction of metal–organic frameworks as precursors/templates for designing metal phosphides is gaining attention due to the inherited beneficial properties of the precursor [27–31]. One such, is Prussian blue analogues (PBAs) [32–35], their well-defined structures and high surface area enables the abundant active sites and the ability to coordinate the binding sites of the tunable metal make it an ideal templates for the reaction to occur [36]. Nevertheless, conductivity remains the most challenge in PBA-derived materials. Concurrently, the latter strategy combines the PBA-derived with the carbon-based materials, as they possess good electrical conductivity.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are flat 0D nanomaterials characterized by an atomically thin graphitic plane [37–40]. The inclusion of numerous edge and defect sites in GQD has facilitated in the hosting of catalytic processes. The carbon frameworks of GQDs can be effectively modified by doping heteroatoms, enhancing their electrocatalytic efficiency and making them prime candidates for water splitting [41]. In recent years, the utilization of quantum dots to improve the performance of the electrocatalyst for hydrogen evolution reactions has been gradually developed [42,43]. Lv et al. prepared nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and Ni3S2 nanosheets on the Ni foam (Ni3S2-NGQDs/NF) via in situ formation [44]. The results demonstrated the introduction of NGQDs to the Ni3S2 can add more active edge sites and change the electronic structure to enhance the conductivity and catalytic performance. On the other hand, Huang et al. utilized GQD to decorate NiCo2S4, which can inhibit the aggregation and dissolution of the NiCo2S4 to maintain the structure stability and further enhance its electrochemical performance [45]. In light of these beneficial attributes, the strategic combination of heteroatom-doped GQD on the PBA-derived phosphides emerges as an intriguing solution to address the long-term conductivity issues of the TMPs.

Inspired by those previous revelations, here to report, a cost-effective and highly active HER electrocatalyst consisting of NGQD decorated on the CoFe phosphide (NGQD/CoFeP). This architecture was built by two-steps, first altering the CoFePBA by NGQD on the nickel foam through a facile coprecipitation process, and then transforming to phosphide by a phosphorization strategy, as shown in the Scheme 1 . The NGQD took a major part on the surface restructuring of the CoFePBA, as well as adjusting the electronic configuration of the bimetal, hence further improves the electron transport and electrocatalytic activity. Specifically, the restructured surface by the NGQD exposed more active sites that beneficial for hosting the reaction. Those unique material combination enables the high HER activity, that exhibits negligible overpotential of 70 mV to achieve 10 mA cm−2 and.

158 mV to achieve 100 mA cm−2 in 0.5 M H2SO4. Furthermore, a remarkable long-term stability was recorded in 600 mA cm−2 for a continuous 12 h of operation. Our findings imply that decorating NGQD on the surface of TMPs allows for surface restructuring and electronic configuration, which subsequently solves the conductivity issue to provide viable electrocatalysts for HER applications.

Cobalt(II)nitrate hexahydrate ((Co(NO3)2·6H2O) ≥ 98 %) and polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP, MW = 58,000) were obtained from Alfa Aesar. Potassium hexacyanoferrate (K3(Fe(CN)6) ≥ 99.0 %) and ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH, 28–30 %) was purchased from J.T. Baker. Trisodium citrate dihydrate ((C6H5Na3O7·2H2O) ≥ 99 %) was used from ACROS and Nafion® 117 solution (∼5%) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Monosodium phosphate (NaH2PO4, ≥98.0 %) was supplied by Sigma-Aldrich. and citric acid ((C6H8O7) ≥ 99.5 %) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, ≥95.0 %) were obtained from Scharlau.

NGQD were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. At first, 40 mg of citric acid was mixed with 5 ml of ammonia hydroxide and 40 ml of deionized water, then putting the mixing solution into the Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After that, the solution was treated hydrothermally for 3 hr at 200 °C. After the reaction was completed, the liquid was centrifuged with a 3 k Da dialysis tube to remove impurities to obtain NGQD.

To make the catalytic catalyst grow more uniformly, the co-precipitation method directly synthesizes the catalysts on the nickel foam. Firstly, 200 mg of potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]) was added to 60 ml of deionized water to form solution A, while 300 mg of Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), 300 mg of polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP), 500 mg of trisodium citrate dihydrate (C6H5Na3O7·2H2O) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots were added to 60 ml of deionized water to comprise solution B. Secondly, the Ni foam was put into solution B, and solution A was added dropwise to solution B. Lastly, put it in an oven to maintain at 80 °C after combining the solution altogether. By the above process, the NGQD/CoFePBA can be obtained and grown on the Ni foam, and then the whole piece of Ni foam containing Prussian blue is placed in a tube furnace. In a tube furnace, the produced NGQD/CoFePBA and NaH2PO2 were distributed in two different positions, with NaH2PO2 on the upstream side. The mass ratio was 1:6. The products were then heated to 350 °C using a 2 °C min−1 heating rate and maintained at this temperature for 3 h in an N2 environment.

To examine the morphology and composition of the prepared nanomaterials, images of field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) were taken using a scanning electron microscope (JSM-6500F, JEOL). A cold field-emission TEM (JEM-2100F, JEOL) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV was used for transmission electron microscopy (TEM). At room temperature, photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was performed on the aqueous as-prepared NGQD dispersion. A commercial spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Nanolog-3 spectrofluorometer) equipped with an InGaAs NIR detector and a 20 nm bandpass for both emission and excitation was used to collect the spectra. The measured excitation power was used to scale the PL spectra. To determine the crystallinity of materials, surface morphology was characterized using an X-ray diffractometer (D2 Phaser, Bruker) with Cu K radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, JPS- 9030, JEOL ) was utilized to analyze the electronic states of the materials. Soft-XAS of the Co L-edge and Fe L-edge was recorded by utilizing the total electron yield (TEY) mode at the BL20A1 beamline, the National Synchrotron Radiation Research Center (NSRRC) in Hsinchu, Taiwan. Co and Fe K-edge X-ray absorption spectroscopy data were acquired on the beamline BL17C1 in NSRRC as well.

All the electrochemical experiments were carried out by an Autolab PGSTAT302N electrochemical workstation in a three-electrode system at room temperature (∼298 K) in 0.5 M H2SO4. As the working electrode, counter electrode, and reference electrode, the materials on Ni foam (1 cm × 1 cm), Pt foil (1 cm × 1 cm), and Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) electrodes were utilized, respectively. The electrocatalytic performance of NF, Pt/C and other comparable electrodes was evaluated for comparison. The reported potentials for the HER were transferred to RHE by using the Nernst equation,
E
R H E
= (
E
A g / A g C l / 3 M
K C l
+
E
A g / A g C l / 3 M
K C l
°
+ 0.059 × pH) V where
E
A g / A g C l / 3 M
K C l
°
=
0.2114 V at 25 °C. And, the overpotential (η) values just equaled to the
E
R H E
because the theoretical potential of the hydrogen evolution reaction is zero. At a sweep rate of 5 mV s−1, Tafel curves and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves were acquired in saturated N2, and both were then shown with iR-correction. By applying a voltage with a 5 mV amplitude over a frequency range of 105 to 10-1 Hz, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results were obtained. Additionally, the 12-hour durability test was carried out with a 600 mA cm−2 current density. For the overall hydrogen evolution reaction test, nickel foam was first washed in ultrasonicator with 1 M HCl, deionized water several times, and then dried in the oven.

The Faraday Efficiency (FE) of NGQD/CoFeP was measured by using an H-type electrolytic cell. The cell comprised of a two-compartment H-cell and a Nafion membrane for separation. The Faradaic efficiency of hydrogen production of NGQD/CoFeP was tested at the cathode. The hydrogen content Vp produced by NGQD/CoFeP was measured by drainage method after working for 30 min at 100 mA cm−2. The theoretical hydrogen production Vt is calculated by the following equation: Where Vt = I * t * 24,500 / F * z, I = current density * electrode area = 0.1 A, t = 300 s (collect data per 5 min), F = 96485C/mol, z = 2. FE = Vp/Vt.

NGQD were synthesized through a hydrothermal method, which used citric acid and ammonia hydroxide as precursors. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images have been used to observe the morphology of samples. In Fig. 1 a, it depicted that the distribution of NGQD was uniform and had a far small particle size. To further confirm the size distribution clearly, the histogram demonstrated the normal particle size distribution, and the particle distribution was between 2.5 and 4.5 nm. Due to the significant quantum effect and edge effect, NGQD revealed distinct photoluminescence (PL) activity that generated intense fluorescence. By recording the emission spectra of the sample after excitation at various excitation wavelengths, the photoluminescence excitation-emission-intensity spectra of NGQD were observed in Fig. 1b. With an excitation wavelength of 380 nm, NGQD exhibited significant emissions around 440 nm from Fig. 1b. The chemical state and composition of the surface of NGQD were further disclosed by XPS, demonstrating the presence of C, N, and O, as shown in Figure S1 . The presence of the N 1s (401 eV) peak in NGQD, together with the O 1s (533 eV) and C 1 s (285 eV) peaks, confirms that N atoms have been doped successfully. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies have been conducted to identify the chemical and environmental changes brought on by N dopants. For C 1 s signal displayed in Fig. 1c, the band is deconvoluted into five peaks at 284.5, 285.2, 286.2, 287.8, and 288.5 eV, contributing to the bindings of CC, CN, CO, CO, and O-CO. This indicates that the N had successfully been doped into the carbon structure. Furthermore, Pyridinic N (399.3 eV), pyrrolic N (400.2 eV), and graphitic N (401.6 eV) were all visible in the high-resolution N 1s spectra of NGQD with different structures in Fig. 1d [46], which implied the NGQD had diverse functional groups in its structure. Additionally, the Raman spectra ( Fig. S1
b) revealed two distinctive spectral features at approximately 1338 and ∼ 1589 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands characteristic, respectively.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images have been utilized to examine the morphology of samples to determine the differences between materials after adding NGQD to the PBA. Before combining with NGQD, the CoFePBA nanocubes had smooth surfaces, and the particle size was around 500 nm to 650 nm as seen in Fig. 2 a. After adding NGQD into the mixing solution, the surface of the nanocubes began to get rough, and the size of particles decreased apparently down to around 300 nm in Fig. 2b. On the other hand, we used soft-XAS data to investigate further information about the surface restructuring effect of CoFePBA by NGQD. In Fig. 2c, the Co l-edge demonstrated that the intensity of low-spin Co3+ (3d) states and Co3+ (4 s) were less intensified upon NGQD inclusion, representing a decrease of the unoccupied electron density states at Co sites within NGQD/CoFePBA, forcefully confirming a higher electron density of Co sites [47]. However, the high-spin Co2+ did not have notable alternation [48]. By contrast, there was no significant difference with the Fe l-edge in Fig. 2d. The intensity variation within the Co l-edge spectra between these two samples was more noticeable than that of Fe, suggesting that the NGQD had a significantly stronger impact on Co than that of Fe.

Furthermore, Co K-edge X-ray absorption near-edge structures (XANES) region was further conducted to evaluate the electronic structure modulation by inducing NGQD (Fig. 3 a). A negligible energy shift between CoFePBA and NGQD/CoFePBA was noticed, which confirmed that the PBA structure was well preserved upon the introduction NGQD, possible confirmation of the surface-only restructuring of PBA [49], in well agreement with XRD results ( Figure S2
). However, the intense intensity was observed on the NGQD/CoFePBA, indicating a gradual oxidation of the Co. On the other hand, there was no significant difference with CoFePBA and NGQD/CoFePBA in Fe K-edge (Fig. 3b). This result was similar to the previous s-XAS l-edge results, proving that the introduction of NGQD into CoFePBA had a greater impact on Co. To be fair, the valence states of both Co and Fe were remained similar to that of PBA (prior transformation), indicating no further oxidation occurred.

Additionally, to analyze the influence of the level of NGQD into the PBA, we put 0.1, 1.0, and 3.0 ml NGQD solution into the mixing solution, respectively. It showed that no matter how much liquid was added, the cubic size of the materials would constantly decrease, as shown in Figure S3 . Among them, 1NGQD/CoFePBA had the smallest size. However, there was no apparent relationship between size and the content of NGQD.

Hollow multivoid nanostructures were seen in Fig. 4
a. The cube-shaped walls and diagonal body direction of the NGQD/CoFeP nanostructure had an open interior void. Moreover, the size of NGQD/CoFeP was around 300 nm. The result was consistence with SEM image in Figure S4 . The varying defect densities in the surface and corner regions should be the cause of such a distinctive hollow structure. Due to the relatively more considerable defects and higher reactivity of these locations, phosphorization of NGQD/CoFePBA nanostructures occurred preferentially on their corners. As the reaction continued, the nanocubes' phosphorization rate in their diagonal body direction accelerated and tended to produce particular hollow voids. The composition and distribution of the NGQD/CoFeP nanocubes were discovered using the EDX elemental mapping. NGQD/CoFeP was successfully formed (Fig. 4 a), as shown in Fig. 4 b to 4e, where corresponding Co, Fe, P, and O elements were found with uniform distribution across the cuboidal geometry. This uniform distribution was further confirmed by the EDX atomic composition ( Table S1 ). It was interesting to note that uniform distributions of the elements C and N are also shown in Fig. 4 f and Fig. 4 g, which may be related to the leftover C and N from the CN– disintegration during the phosphorization process at 350 °C. The XRD pattern revealed the high crystallinity of as-obtained CoFePBA and NGQD/CoFePBA in Figure S4 . Their peak distribution was consistent with K2CoFe(CN)6, which indicated the PBA synthesized successfully and had high crystallinity. Additionally, the peak intensity of NGQD/CoFePBA was a little decreased, meaning PBA decorated with NGQD would decline in crystallinity of PBA. On the other hand, after phosphorization, the crystallinity of the sample drastically decreased, becoming more amorphous. NGQD/CoFeP was with a few peaks distinct at 2θ = 31.9, 36.5, 48.3, and 56.8, similar to CoP and FeP, both having similar crystallographic features to each other. All of the above indicated the successful syntheses of bimetallic phosphide.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of transition metal phosphide was characterized to confirm their electronic structure and states further. The overall spectrum of NGQD/CoFeP affirmed the coexistence of Co, Fe, and P elements, as shown in Fig. 5 a, which matched well with previous EDX results in Fig. 4. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Co 2p was depicted in Fig. 5b.

The binding energies peaks at 778.2 and 793.1 eV could be assigned to Co–P, whereas the binding energy peaks at 781.3 (2p3/2) and 797.3 eV (2p1/2) coincided with Co–O [50]. The existence of the CoP suggests the successful transformation to the transition metal phosphides, in parallel, a crucial contribution to the catalytic performance as the metal phosphides’ surface provides suitable binding sites for hydrogen—the HER intermediates [51]. Furthermore, the peaks appearing 785.3 and 802.1 eV corresponded to the shakeup satellite peaks of Co 2p [52]. These observations confirmed the existence of Co elements in the forms Co2+ and Co3+. In the Fe 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 5c), the binding energies of 712.7 and 725.2 eV corresponded to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively, indicating the presence of Fe2+. The 706.6 eV and 720.4 eV peaks indicated the presence of Fe–P in the NGQD/CoFeP catalyst. Identically, the metal phosphide unique surface enables the ideal spot for the molecular hydrogen binding sites, tuning the overall reaction kinetics. Another singlet Fe 2p peaks of 709.5 eV was associated with the Fe–O species produced by the surface oxidation [53]. Metal phosphides were detected in the XPS spectrum of P 2p (Fig. 5d), with two peaks at 128.9 and 129.6 eV. The binding energy peak at 133.8 and 136.2 eV was from PO bonding, which could be due to the surface oxidation of metal phosphides [54,55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic activities of CoFe PBA with various NGQD amount toward hydrogen evolution reaction
ce:section.ce:para: After the NGQD was successfully converted into CoFePBA, optimizing the amount of NGQD added into the solution was essential. Because of that, we individually added 0.1, 1, 3, and 5 ml of NGQD to PBA for comparison. Fig. 6 a presented that HER performance had significantly increased compared with the sample without NGQD. Additionally, 1NGQD/CoFePBA exhibits the best electrocatalytic ability for HER among them, and it displays 169 and 303 mV at the current density of 10 and 100 mA cm−2. In order to investigate the kinetics of materials HER performances, the Tafel plots are calculated from LSV curves. As shown in Fig. 6b, the linear regions of the Tafel plots are fitted to the Tafel equation. 1NGQD/CoFePBA shows the high HER catalytic kinetic characteristics among them, and its Tafel slope is 148 mV dec-1. In addition, Fig. 6c shows the Nyquist plots, 1NGQD/CoFePBA also exhibits smaller charge transfer resistance (Rct) than other samples. From Table S2 , it can be seen more clearly that the overpotential at a current density of 10 and 100 mA cm−2 and the Tafel slope of each sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of NGQD/CoFeP for hydrogen evolution reaction
ce:section.ce:para: On the other hand, the HER activity of CoFeP, 0.1NGQD/CoFeP, 1NGQD/CoFeP, and 3NGQD/CoFeP was investigated on Ni foam in 0.5 M H2SO4 aqueous solution. The polarization curves for the HER on four different materials are shown in Fig. S5a. In comparison to other materials that contain NGQD decorated CoFeP, the CoFeP has the lowest catalytic activity with an overpotential of 82 mV at 10 mA cm−2, indicating that converting NGQD into CoFeP improves HER performances. Furthermore, 3NGQD/CoFeP has the highest catalytic performance with a lower overpotential of 70 mV at 10 mA cm−2 than 1NGQD/CoFeP (74 mV) and 0.1NGQD/CoFeP (78 mV). Fig. S5b demonstrates that 3NGQD/CoFeP has a lower Tafel slope of 56 mV dec-1 than 1NGQD/CoFeP (66 mV dec-1), 0.1NGQD/CoFeP (66 mV dec-1), and CoFeP (73 mV dec-1). The EIS result also exhibits the same trend in Fig. S5c. From Table S3 , it can be seen more clearly that the overpotential at a current density of 10 and 100 mA cm−2 and the Tafel slope of each sample. Furthermore, the improved kinetics associated with the exposed active sites are validated by estimating the Cdl, which is used to represent the relative ECSA of the catalysts. In brief, the potential is scanned at various scan rates (20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 mV s−1) in a non-faradaic potential window. The CV curves of CoFeP, 0.1NGQD/CoFeP, 0.1NGQD/CoFeP, and 3NGQD/CoFeP are shown in Figure S6 . 3NGQD/CoFeP has a higher Cdl of 71.8 mF cm−2 than CoFeP (56.3 mF cm−2), 0.1NGQD/CoFeP (58.7 mF cm−2), and 1NGQD/CoFeP (66.3 mF cm−2), indicating more electrochemically active metal sites exposed on the catalyst surface and thus the highest HER catalytic activity.

The HER activity of CoFePBA, NGQD/CoFePBA, CoFeP, NGQD/CoFeP, and commercial 20 % Pt/C catalysts was measured in 0.5 M H2SO4 solutions using a typical three-electrode system. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of all samples are conducted at saturated N2 and room temperature using a 5 mV s−1 scan rate in Fig. 7 a. The 20 % Pt/C catalyst has the maximum activity when used as a HER electrocatalyst, and the overpotentials are minimal at 31 mV in 0.5 M H2SO4 at the current density of 10 mA cm−2 (η10). In comparison to CoFeP 82 mV, NGQD/CoFePBA 169 mV, and CoFePBA 220 mV, the HER catalytic activity for NGQD/CoFeP supports a more excellent HER activity with η10 = 70 mV in 0.5 M H2SO4. The HER electrochemical reaction kinetics are directly correlated with the Tafel slope value. On the basis of the Tafel equation (η = b log j + a, where η is the overpotential, j is the current density, b is the Tafel slope, and a is determined by j and b), the LSV curves were used to produce the Tafel plots. The lower slope implies a quicker reaction rate, which shows faster electron transport. As shown in Fig. 7b, NGQD/CoFeP showed a smaller Tafel slope of 56 mV dec−1 than CoFeP (73 mV dec−1), NGQD/CoFePBA (148 mV dec−1), and CoFePBA (181 mV dec−1), which indicated the high catalytic kinetics of NGQD/CoFeP. To provide more information on the kinetics of the electrode reactions in the as-prepared materials, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was also conducted to determine the electrical conductivity of the electrodes. In Fig. 7c, the charge transfer resistance (Rct) at the electrode and electrolyte contact is primarily responsible for the observed semicircle. NGQD/CoFeP showed a significantly smaller semicircular diameter in the EIS than CoFeP, NGQD/CoFePBA, and CoFePBA, indicating the fast charge transfer kinetics occurring at the interface of NGQD/CoFeP and electrolyte, thus contributing to the excellent HER performance. In addition, the trends found in the HER polarization curves, Tafel slopes, and the EIS data were in good agreement. From Table S4 , it can be seen more clearly that the overpotential at a current density of 10 and 100 mA cm−2 and the Tafel slope of each sample. However, we could see our material had a closer performance to commercial Pt/C at a high current density. Moreover, a chronopotentiometry test was then performed to evaluate the stability of NGQD/CoFeP at a constant current density of 600 mA cm−2. As shown in Fig. 7d, it demonstrated remarkable HER durability in such high current density. It could retain a potential around −0.3 V (vs. RHE) for 12 h with almost little decay, further proving its exceptional stability. Furthermore, the continuous CV cycle ( Figure S7 ) unveiled an impressively consistent electrochemical profile of the NGQD/CoFeP, supporting its extraordinary stability. To delve into the alterations in chemical states, we conducted an extensive XPS analysis of the NGQD/CoFePBA catalyst after the stability testing, as shown in the Figure S8 (ESI). Of particular interest, the overall XPS spectra provided unequivocal confirmation regarding the coexistence of key elemental constituents, such as cobalt (Co), iron (Fe), and phosphorus (P), even subsequent to the long-term stability test. This coexistence of elemental presence highlights the catalyst's extraordinary durability throughout the experimental conditions. In parallel, the high-scan XPS spectra of Co 2p and Fe 2p exhibited identical deconvolution spectra features even after the stability test in a harsh condition, indicating the remarkable durability of the NGQD/CoFePBA. However, some noticeable changes were observed in the high-resolution XPS 2p spectra, where only singular peak was observed in phosphide spectra (128.8 eV), and the PO bonding peaks appears to be stronger. This data signifies a transformative phenomenon, that with increasing reaction time, the phosphide bond weakens and the PO bonding increases, suggesting that the phosphide has been oxidized and converted to phosphate during the HER.

To measure the purity of H2 gas and calculate the Faradaic efficiency, NGQD/CoFeP was conducted in the H-type cell to do the overall water splitting seen in Fig. 8 a and Video S1 . The left side was operating OER, and the right was working HER. In the device, a Nafion membrane helped proton transport and separated two chambers to prevent the gas flux in the middle. When the water splitting began, we collected the hydrogen by draining water gathering of gas law. After that, the collected gas was transported to gas chromatography (GC) to detect the gas composition in Fig. 8b. The result showed the high purity of hydrogen, which illustrated no side reaction. Furthermore, The Faradaic efficiency (FE) of NGQD/CoFeP determined by the ratio of evolved gas amount and theoretic one in the chronoamperometry process is close to 100 % in acidic media for hydrogen production in Fig. 8c.

In summary, a delicate design of a HER catalyst with complete reconfiguration features through surface restructuring CoFePBA by NGQD, and further transformed it to CoFe phosphide for a high-current density range. According to experimental observations, SEM and TEM evidenced the successful surface reconfiguration of the PBA by NGQD, while the XAS provide solid proof of the electronic states reconfiguration of the bimetallic CoFe. By far, the NGQD created interspaces on the surface of the PBA and partially fill the gap of the unoccupied electron state within the metal, enhance the electron transfer ability. Benefitting those distinctive features, a remarkable electrocatalytic properties was achieved that originating from the intrinsic reconfiguration that led to the enhanced conductivity. Our finding marked that the as-synthesized NGQD/CoFeP in 0.5 M H2SO4 have overpotentials of 70 mV and 158 mV at 10 and 100 mA cm−2, respectively. In comparison to prior study in acidic solution in Table S5 , NGQD/CoFeP outperformed numerous transition metal phosphides substantially. Yet, it was also capable of assessing the minimal charge transfer resistance and low Tafel slope. Furthermore, it maintained a strong stability under an extreme current density at 600 mA cm−2 for over 12 h. Our findings point toward surface restructuring CoFePBA-derived TMPs by NGQD as a viable electrocatalyst for the hydrogen evolution process because of their high current density suitability and ability to maximize electrocatalytic activity.

W.S. Lin, M. Rinawati, and W.H. Huang contributed equally to this work. The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

Wei-Shiang Lin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Mia Rinawati: Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Wei-Hsiang Huang: Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Chia-Yu Chang: Investigation. Ling-Yu Chang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yao-Sheng Cheng: Investigation, Validation. Ching-Cheng Chang: Investigation, Validation. Jeng-Lung Chen: Investigation, Methodology. Wei-Nien Su: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Min-Hsin Yeh: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) in Taiwan (MOST 111-2221-E-011-011, NSTC 112-2218-E-01-011, and NSTC 112-2221-E-011-016-MY3), the Ministry of Education of Taiwan (“Sustainable Electrochemical Energy Development (SEED) Center” project) and the National Taiwan University of Science and Technology & Institut Teknologi Sepuluh Nopember Joint Research Program (ITS-NTUST-112-05). Research facilities of hard-XAS and soft-XAS were provided by the beamline of BL17C1 and BL20A1, respectively, National Synchrotron Radiation Research Center (NSRRC) in Taiwan. We also thank Ms. C.-Y. Chien of NSTC (located at National Taiwan University) for assistance with the TEM experiments.
